issues.description: Promises 'predict offspring ratios for almost any cross' by the end — but the lesson never actually walks through a dihybrid Punnett square, a test cross, or a sex-linked pedigree. The pause problem only covers monohybrid + ABO. Either lower the promise or add a worked dihybrid example.

Says 'Meiosis Two is equational — sister chromatids separate, just like mitosis, but on haploid cells.' This invites the misconception that Meiosis II = mitosis. It's NOT mitosis: the sister chromatids are no longer identical because of crossing over in Prophase I. A student building a flashcard 'Meiosis II = mitosis' would lose points.

'Each homologous pair lines up independently, giving 2^23' — the script never explains *why* 23 (it's the haploid number, not the diploid). A new learner could think 2^46. One extra sentence ('one exponent per homologous pair, and humans have 23') would close the gap.

Mendel's laws are stated, but the connection to meiosis is implicit. Best AP free-response answers explicitly link 'segregation happens because homologs separate in Anaphase I' and 'independent assortment happens because homolog pairs orient randomly in Metaphase I.' Currently a learner has to make that bridge alone.

Introduces dihybrid 9:3:3:1 but does NOT show how it is derived (3:1 x 3:1, or a 4x4 Punnett) or what the four phenotype classes correspond to. A student who memorizes '9:3:3:1' without understanding why will fail any FRQ that varies the cross (e.g., test cross of dihybrid heterozygote, which gives 1:1:1:1).

The contrast 'blended versus both-showing' is great, but the incomplete-dominance ratio (1:2:1) is stated as if it's the phenotype ratio without flagging that for incomplete dominance phenotype ratio = genotype ratio. New learners often miss this is the key reason 1:2:1 shows up as a phenotype ratio.

Says 'a gene has more than two versions in the population, even though any one person carries only two.' Good. But does not address the dominance hierarchy inside ABO (I^A and I^B codominant, both dominant over i). A learner could think 'I^A I^B' is type A or type B and get the AB phenotype wrong.

'Sex-linked — most often X-linked recessive.' True statistically, but the script never mentions X-linked dominant or Y-linked, and never gives the pedigree-reading rules (affected father → all daughters carriers, etc.). The lesson explicitly markets sex-linked pedigrees as a 'test-day trap' in section 16 but never teaches the pedigree skill. Big gap.

Skin color is given as a polygenic example, but no mention that polygenic traits often look like a bell curve (and that this is HOW you recognize them on the exam — continuous distribution). Half-explained.

Says 'the genotype-to-phenotype ratio is 1:2:1 for both.' This is misleading: for incomplete dominance phenotype = 1:2:1, for codominance phenotype is also 1:2:1 in a single-gene case — true — but the language 'genotype-to-phenotype ratio' is non-standard and may confuse. Should say 'both produce a 1:2:1 phenotype ratio because the heterozygote is its own visible class.'

Recap introduces nondisjunction, aneuploidy, Down/Klinefelter/Turner for the FIRST time — these were never taught in the body of the lesson. A recap should recap, not introduce. Either move chromosomal disorders into their own section earlier, or drop from recap.
No mention of epistasis, even though it's in the AP CED Unit 5 reference and is a common FRQ topic (Labrador coat color). Glaring omission for a comprehensive Mendelian-genetics module.

Linkage and recombination are alluded to in section 8 ('if they're linked, the ratio breaks') but recombination frequency and centimorgan mapping (in the CED) are never explained. Top students preparing for FRQs on gene mapping will not get what they need here.

summary: Script is engaging and covers the headline concepts, but builds three real misconception risks: (1) Meiosis II framed as 'just mitosis,' (2) dihybrid 9:3:3:1 memorized without derivation, and (3) sex-linked pedigrees teased as a trap but never taught. Recap also smuggles in untaught chromosomal disorders. Solid foundation, but a learner aiming for a 5 needs more worked examples and the missing topics (epistasis, recombination frequency, pedigree rules).
